label: Cartesian mechanism and dualism

limitations: Draft awaiting human domain review (D2-001); review needed before any scholarly authority is claimed.
Scoped to Descartes himself; occasionalist successors (Malebranche) and wider mechanical philosophy are out of scope.
Interpretive disputes flagged by the sources include the ontology of force, the Cartesian Circle, and the extent of animal sentience denial.

No claims are recorded for origin, time, fundamental_problem, transformation, or destiny as separate primitives: the sources used cover physics and metaphysics of mind, not cosmogony or soteriology. Absence means unspecified, not denial.

claims.claim: Created reality divides into two kinds of substance: 'the essence of mind is thought' and 'the essence of matter is extension in length, breadth, and depth', with God as the infinite substance sustaining both.

Body just is extension, and the world is a plenum: 'the extension in length, breadth, and depth which constitutes the space occupied by a body, is exactly the same as that which constitutes the body', and since 'all places are full of bodies…. it follows that no body can move except in a complete circle'.

All natural phenomena are explained mechanically — by 'motion, size, shape, and the arrangement of their parts' — without substantial forms or goal-directed properties; teleology is excluded from physics.

The laws of motion rest on God: 'each thing, as far as is in its power, always remains in the same state; and that consequently, when it is once moved, it always continues to move', because God 'conserves motion; not always contained in the same parts of matter, but transferred from some parts to others depending on the ways in which they come in contact'.
claims.notes: Whether force is a real property of bodies or shorthand for God's continuous action is a standing scholarly dispute (Garber vs. Gabbey, per the SEP entry), tied to debates over relational motion and the definition of body.

claims.claim: The heavens are fluid vortices: planetary orbits are explained by 'a large circling band of material particles', the planets being 'carried along by the heaven'.

The thinking self is really distinct from body and better known than it: 'I think, therefore I am'; 'I could pretend that I had no body... I could not... pretend that I did not exist' — though 'if mind is unextended and matter is extended, how do they interact?' remains the acknowledged difficulty.
Humans alone unite mind and mechanism: 'Descartes regarded nonhuman animals as machines, devoid of mind and consciousness, and hence lacking in sentience'.
claims.notes: Per the SEP entry, 'recent scholars question whether Descartes denied all feeling to animals'; the strength of the animal-machine doctrine is contested.

claims.claim: Secure knowledge of nature is underwritten by a non-deceiving God: 'it is clear enough from this that he cannot be a deceiver, since it is manifest by the natural light that all fraud and deception depend on some defect'.
claims.notes: The Cartesian Circle — whether clear and distinct perception can non-circularly certify God, who in turn certifies clear and distinct perception — is a classic contested point flagged by the entry.

distinguishing_claims: Mind (thought) and body (extension) are really distinct substances
Nature is a plenum of matter in motion explained without final causes
The laws of motion derive from God's immutable conservation
Animals are machines; only humans join mind to mechanism
common_confusions: Cartesian dualism pairs mind with body, unlike Sāṃkhya's consciousness–nature dualism where intellect belongs to nature.
Descartes's mechanism retains God at its foundation: it is not scientific naturalism.
Excluding teleology from physics does not deny God's purposes — it denies their explanatory role in natural science.

sources.title: René Descartes (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.passage: the essence of mind is thought (and) the essence of matter is extension in length, breadth, and depth (Princ. I, II.3–4). [...] I think, therefore I am (7:140). [...] I could pretend that I had no body... I could not... pretend that I did not exist (Discourse, 6:32–3). [...] if mind is unextended and matter is extended, how do they interact? (3:694). [...] Descartes regarded nonhuman animals as machines, devoid of mind and consciousness, and hence lacking in sentience. [...] It is clear enough from this that he cannot be a deceiver, since it is manifest by the natural light that all fraud and deception depend on some defect (7:52). (First published 2008-12-03; substantive revision 2023-10-23. Author byline not captured at access time — verify on review.)

sources.title: Descartes' Physics (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.author: Edward Slowik
sources.passage: the extension in length, breadth, and depth which constitutes the space occupied by a body, is exactly the same as that which constitutes the body (Pr II 10). [...] all places are full of bodies…. From this it follows that no body can move except in a complete circle (Pr II 33). [...] motion, size, shape, and the arrangement of their parts (suffice to explain all natural phenomena) (AT XI 25–26). [...] each thing, as far as is in its power, always remains in the same state; and that consequently, when it is once moved, it always continues to move (Pr II 37). [...] He conserves motion; not always contained in the same parts of matter, but transferred from some parts to others depending on the ways in which they come in contact (Pr II 62). [...] a large circling band of material particles (explains planetary orbits) (Pr III 28, 48–54). (First published 2005-07-29; substantive revision 2025-10-20.)

review.notes: Drafted for D2-001 batch 5. Source URLs fetched and passages captured on 2026-07-06. Review needed: no human domain review yet. Open provenance item: the SEP 'René Descartes' author byline was not captured during fetch and must be added at review.
